reference: GATE CS — Discrete Mathematics / Graph Theory

map.condition: Question involves vertex degrees or degree sum
Question asks whether graph has a planar drawing
Question asks about isomorphism conditions to check
Question involves connectivity number or cut sets

formulas.name: Handshaking Lemma
formulas.plain: Sum of all vertex degrees = 2 * |E|
formulas.whenToUse: Counting edges from degree information, or proving a degree sequence is valid.

terms.means: number of edges incident to vertex v
number of edges in the graph

formulas.trap: Each edge contributes 2 to the degree sum (once per endpoint), so the sum is always even — an odd number of odd-degree vertices is impossible.

formulas.name: Even Number of Odd-Degree Vertices
formulas.plain: The number of vertices with odd degree is always even.
formulas.whenToUse: Validating a degree sequence or proving a specific degree configuration is impossible.
formulas.trap: A sequence with an odd count of odd-degree values can never be a valid graph degree sequence.

formulas.name: Edges in a k-Regular Graph
formulas.whenToUse: Computing number of edges when every vertex has exactly the same degree k.
formulas.trap: For k*|V| to be even (giving integer edges), either k or |V| must be even; otherwise a k-regular graph on |V| vertices cannot exist.

formulas.name: Degree Bounds in Simple Graph
formulas.plain: In any simple graph with |V|>1 vertices, at least two vertices share the same degree.
formulas.whenToUse: Proving two vertices must have equal degree (classic GATE MCQ).
formulas.trap: Degree 0 and degree |V|-1 cannot both appear simultaneously (isolated vertex and fully-connected vertex cannot coexist in a simple graph), so pigeonhole guarantees a repeated degree.

formulas.name: Weighted Degree Sum xi(G)
formulas.plain: xi(G) = sum over d of (count of degree-d vertices * d) = 2|E|
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing xi values of two trees or graphs to deduce edge / vertex relationships (GATE 2014 question on trees S and T).

terms.means: number of vertices of degree d

formulas.trap: For trees, |E| = |V| - 1, so xi(T) = 2(|V|-1). Equal xi implies equal |V| (and thus equal |E| for trees).

formulas.name: Connectivity Hierarchy
formulas.plain: vertex-connectivity <= edge-connectivity <= minimum degree
formulas.whenToUse: Bounding or comparing vertex/edge connectivity and minimum degree.

terms.means: vertex connectivity — min vertices to remove to disconnect G
edge connectivity — min edges to remove to disconnect G

formulas.trap: kappa = kappa' = delta for complete graphs K_n (all equal n-1), which is the maximum possible.

formulas.name: Complete Graph Connectivity
formulas.plain: For K_n: vertex-connectivity = edge-connectivity = min-degree = n-1
formulas.whenToUse: Evaluating connectivity of complete graphs.

terms.means: degree (and connectivity) of each vertex in K_n

formulas.trap: K_n is maximally connected; removing any single vertex still leaves K_{n-1}, so you need n-1 removals to disconnect.

formulas.name: Bridge and Articulation Point Definitions
formulas.latex: \text{Bridge: edge } e \text{ s.t. } G - e \text{ has more components than } G\\ \text{Cut vertex: vertex } v \text{ s.t. } G - v \text{ has more components than } G
formulas.plain: Bridge = edge whose removal increases connected components. Cut vertex = vertex whose removal increases components.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying critical edges/vertices in connectivity questions.

terms.means: graph G with edge e removed
graph G with vertex v and all its incident edges removed

formulas.trap: Every edge in a tree is a bridge. A graph with no bridges is called 2-edge-connected.

formulas.name: Adjacency Matrix Properties
formulas.plain: A[i][j] = 1 iff edge (i,j) exists; matrix is symmetric for undirected graphs.
formulas.whenToUse: Writing or verifying adjacency matrix for undirected graphs; checking symmetry.

terms.means: number of walks of length k from i to j

formulas.trap: Self-loops add 2 to diagonal (or 1 depending on convention); simple graphs have 0 diagonal entries. Row sum = degree of vertex.

formulas.name: Connected Components and Spanning Trees
formulas.latex: \text{G is connected} \Leftrightarrow \exists \text{ spanning tree of } G\\ \text{Min edges for connected graph on } n \text{ vertices} = n-1
formulas.plain: G connected iff it has a spanning tree; minimum edges = n-1.
formulas.whenToUse: Determining minimum edges needed, or checking connectivity via spanning tree existence.

terms.means: minimum number of edges for a connected graph (= tree)

formulas.trap: A graph with n vertices and exactly n-1 edges is connected if and only if it is also acyclic (i.e., it is a tree).

formulas.name: Necessary Conditions for Isomorphism
formulas.latex: G_1 \cong G_2 \Rightarrow \begin{cases} |V(G_1)| = |V(G_2)| \\ |E(G_1)| = |E(G_2)| \\ \text{same degree sequence} \\ \text{same number of cycles of each length} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: Isomorphic graphs must have equal |V|, |E|, same sorted degree sequence, and same cycle structure.
formulas.whenToUse: Ruling out isomorphism by checking these invariants (they are necessary but NOT sufficient).

terms.means: sorted list of all vertex degrees

formulas.trap: Identical degree sequences do NOT guarantee isomorphism (e.g., two non-isomorphic graphs can share a degree sequence).

formulas.name: Isomorphism Definition
formulas.latex: G_1 \cong G_2 \Leftrightarrow \exists f: V(G_1) \to V(G_2) \text{ bijection s.t. } (u,v) \in E(G_1) \Leftrightarrow (f(u), f(v)) \in E(G_2)
formulas.plain: G1 isomorphic to G2 iff there exists a bijection f on vertices that preserves all edges.
formulas.whenToUse: Formally verifying or constructing an isomorphism between two graphs.

terms.means: edge sets of graphs G1, G2

formulas.trap: You must verify BOTH directions: every edge in G1 maps to G2 AND every edge in G2 is the image of some edge in G1.

formulas.name: Self-Complementary Cycle
formulas.plain: The cycle graph C_n is isomorphic to its complement only when n = 5.
formulas.whenToUse: Answering GATE question: 'For which n is C_n isomorphic to its complement?'

terms.means: complement of C_n (edges present iff not in C_n)

formulas.trap: C_5 complement is also a C_5 (the Petersen inner pentagram), so they are isomorphic. For other small n, the complement has a different structure.

formulas.name: Petersen Graph Key Properties
formulas.plain: Petersen graph: 10 vertices, 15 edges, 3-regular, girth 5, non-planar, non-Hamiltonian.
formulas.whenToUse: Answering properties of the Petersen graph (standard GATE question on isomorphism and structure).
formulas.trap: The Petersen graph is non-planar and non-Hamiltonian despite being highly symmetric — do not confuse it with a planar graph just because it can be drawn with 5 crossings minimized.

formulas.name: Euler's Formula for Planar Graphs
formulas.plain: V - E + F = 2, where V = vertices, E = edges, F = faces (including outer face).
formulas.whenToUse: Computing number of faces, or deriving edge bounds for planar graphs.

terms.means: number of faces (regions), including the unbounded outer face

formulas.trap: Formula applies only to CONNECTED planar graphs. For disconnected planar graphs: V - E + F = 1 + C, where C = number of connected components.

formulas.name: Edge Bound for Planar Graphs
formulas.plain: E <= 3*V - 6 for any simple planar graph with V >= 3.
formulas.whenToUse: Proving a graph is non-planar by showing it violates this bound.
formulas.trap: This is a necessary condition for planarity, NOT sufficient. If E > 3V-6, graph is definitely non-planar; if E <= 3V-6, graph may still be non-planar.

formulas.name: Edge Bound for Planar Bipartite Graphs
formulas.plain: E <= 2*V - 4 for any simple planar bipartite graph with V >= 3.
formulas.whenToUse: Stronger bound when graph is bipartite — useful to show K_{3,3} is non-planar.
formulas.trap: K_{3,3} has V=6, E=9; bound gives 2*6-4=8 < 9, confirming non-planarity. The stronger bound only applies when you know the graph is bipartite.

formulas.name: Kuratowski's Theorem
formulas.latex: G \text{ is planar} \Leftrightarrow G \text{ contains no subdivision of } K_5 \text{ or } K_{3,3}
formulas.plain: A graph is planar iff it has no subgraph that is a subdivision (homeomorphic copy) of K_5 or K_{3,3}.
formulas.whenToUse: Characterising planarity exactly — the fundamental theorem of graph planarity (GATE direct question).

terms.means: complete bipartite graph on 3+3 vertices (non-planar obstruction)
graph obtained by inserting vertices on edges (homeomorphic subgraph)

formulas.trap: The theorem uses 'subdivision' (homeomorphic subgraph), NOT 'isomorphic subgraph'. Answer C in GATE question on planarity is the correct phrasing.

formulas.name: Non-Planarity of K_5 and K_{3,3}
formulas.latex: K_5: V=5, E=10,\; 3V-6=9 < 10 \Rightarrow \text{non-planar}\\ K_{3,3}: V=6, E=9,\; 2V-4=8 < 9 \Rightarrow \text{non-planar}
formulas.plain: K_5 non-planar: 10 edges > 3*5-6=9. K_{3,3} non-planar: 9 edges > 2*6-4=8.
formulas.whenToUse: Quick verification that K_5 and K_{3,3} violate planarity bounds.
formulas.trap: These are the two minimal non-planar graphs. Every non-planar graph contains a subdivision of at least one of them.

formulas.name: Planarity Testing — Linear Time
formulas.latex: \text{Planarity testing can be done in } O(V + E) \text{ time (e.g., Hopcroft-Tarjan algorithm, 1974)}
formulas.plain: There exists a linear-time O(V+E) algorithm for planarity testing — the statement is TRUE.
formulas.whenToUse: GATE question: 'Is there a linear-time algorithm for testing planarity?' Answer: TRUE.

terms.means: linear time in vertices and edges

formulas.trap: Many students think planarity testing is harder (e.g., polynomial but not linear). The Hopcroft-Tarjan and LR-Planarity algorithms achieve O(n) time.

formulas.name: 3-Cube (Q3) is Planar
formulas.latex: Q_3: V=8,\; E=12,\; 3V-6=18 \geq 12 \Rightarrow \text{not ruled out by bound; } Q_3 \text{ is planar}
formulas.plain: Q3 hypercube: 8 vertices, 12 edges; passes 3V-6=18>=12 bound and is indeed planar.
formulas.whenToUse: GATE question about which graphs among {K_{3,3}-like, Q3} are planar — Q3 is planar.

terms.means: 3-dimensional hypercube graph (2 squares connected by edges)

formulas.trap: Passing the edge bound does not prove planarity, but Q3 can be drawn as a planar graph (square inside square with connecting edges).
